Please describe your research career to date
Research Experience
Health Education England/National Institute for Health and Care Research Pre-doctoral Clinical Academic
Fellowship:
I completed a Health Education England (HEE)/National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Pre-
doctoral Clinical Academic Fellowship (PCAF) in 2022 and undertook a Master of Research (MRes) in Speech,
Language, and Cognition at University College London (UCL). I was awarded a distinction and named on the
Dean’s List for outstanding academic achievement. During this fellowship, I completed a scoping review and a
qualitative research activity.
Scoping Review
This scoping review investigated the feeding and swallowing outcomes of ventilator-assisted children. I was
responsible

secarb__001_b.text:
for formulating the research question, planning the protocol, designing and implementing a search
strategy, compiling and screening literature, extracting data and synthesising findings. I gained valuable skills in
reviewing and appraising the evidence-base, and have a thorough understanding of available evidence and gaps
within my research topic. I am lead author on two resulting publications: a scoping review protocol and a scoping
review1,2.
Qualitative Research
This research activity investigated the perspectives and experiences of feeding and swallowing of parents of
ventilator-assisted children. This was conducted in collaboration with WellChild charity. My roles included
formulating the research question, designing methodology, obtaining ethical and data protection approval,
conducting and transcribing interviews, and analysing data. I am lead author on a publication resulting from this
work3.
Research dissemination and impact
I have disseminated my PCAF findings across national and international forums. I have established collaborative
links with clinical and research teams at Evelina London Children’s Hospital, Pan-Thames Paediatric Long-Term
Ventilation Network and WellCh

secarb__001_c.text:
ild charity. I presented to the Council for Allied Health Professions Research (50
audience members) and the National Allied Health Professionals Long-Term Ventilation network (60 audience
members) in the United Kingdom (UK). I disseminated findings to the Paediatric Dysphagia Interest Group (over
90 participants) in Australia. My research was featured in a blog post in the Feeding Matters conference which was
held virtually from the United States and streamed worldwide.
Other methodologies and publications:
In 2010, I conducted a qualitative study during my Honours degree exploring the views of health professionals on
communication support for children with cerebral palsy. In 2014, alongside my clinical role, I was a research
assistant at Westmead Children’s Hospital in Australia. I worked on a study investigating the applicability of
telehealth in speech and language therapy assessments. I used qualitative and quantitative methods to collect
survey data, conduct patient assessments, design and complete inter-rater reliability tools, and disseminate
findings. I co-authored on publications during my Honours degree in 2014 and research assistant work in 20164,5.
Research training:
I

secarb__001_d.text:
n 2020, I completed the Designing Clinical Research course through King’s Health Partners and University of
California San Francisco. This three-month course provided training on designing research questions, collating
background information, planning subjects/sample size, study methodology, results analysis, statistics and ethics. I
designed, wrote and presented a complete research proposal.
During my MRes, I gained foundational skills in professional research practice which provided me with essential
preparation for undertaking a Doctor of Philosophy (PhD). During this time, I attended external training courses
including the UCL Hospital Biomedical Sciences Patient and Public (PPI) Involvement workshop and the UCL
Academic Communication Centre writing course. I have completed peer reviewer training and International
Council on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements good clinical practice training.
Research networks:
I am on the committee for the National Allied Health Professionals Long-Term Ventilation Group. This involves
regular coordination of professional development opportunities for network members. I have presented education
and training to this network on several occasi

secarb__001_e.text:
ons. I am a member of the Paediatric Dysphagia Speech and
Language Therapists in Research network. My membership in NIHR Ear, Nose and Throat Speech and Language
Therapy Champions provides me with support from NIHR research leaders. Mentoring is provided through my
hospital trust’s doctoral peer support group. I am a reviewer for the NIHR Evidence Alerts database and actively
peer-review for Joanna Briggs Institute Evidence Synthesis.
Professional Experience
I have been a speech and language therapist for 14 years and am the respiratory lead in my team at Evelina
London Children’s Hospital. This involves providing services to children with a variety of feeding and
communication difficulties. I specialise in respiratory diseases, tracheostomy and ventilation. I enjoy working with a
variety of multidisciplinary teams and look forward to extending my scope of practice to become a clinical
academic leader in this area.
Over my career, I have taken part in supervision, clinical education, service evaluation and department-based
research projects in keeping with the National Health Service clinical governance framework. I have used my
research skills to guide evidence-based practice, cli

secarb__001_f.text:
nical decision making and professional development. I have
led quality improvement projects including the development of an inpatient long-term ventilation service and
adaptation of this service during the Coronavirus disease pandemic.
Career trajectory:
During the PCAF, I thoroughly enjoyed working collaboratively with families and stakeholders to address research
priorities. I feel compelled to continue advocating for ventilator-assisted children and their families. I have
continued to engage with parents involved in my qualitative study who have since formed part of my PPI group.
Their engagement and contributions have been valuable in the preparation of this research proposal and
fellowship application. My publications have inspired discussion in national and international teams who have
invited me to present about my research outcomes (see ‘research dissemination and impact’ above). I have also
provided support and mentoring to other health professionals who are seeking clinical academic careers.
During the PhD, I will explore current research gaps in the topic of feeding and swallowing in ventilator-assisted
children. I am determined to drive robust evidence-based initiatives

secarb__001_g.text:
in the UK and abroad, and will continue to
develop local and international research networks. I am committed to expanding my clinical and leadership skills in
order to become an expert practitioner academic. I am excited to continue my clinical academic career in which my
specialist practice is intertwined with a lifelong commitment to research.

Please use this question to detail any contextual factors you wish to make the Selection Committee aware of.
NIHR wants to know about any circumstances so that they may take them into consideration during the
assessment of your application. Contextual factors may include:
 Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
 Reduced time spent undertaking research due to a disability or caring responsibilities. This could include
any physical or mental difficulty that may have impacted your research career. These are situations that
have a significant impact on your ability to undertake research.
 Reduced opportunities to career support e.g. mentorship, and l

imited opportunities to undertake prior
research and training.
 Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career.
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding or fewer publications. In general terms,
mitigating factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that the information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with
General Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).
I took 12 months of maternity leave between December 2022 to December 2023. During this time, I used my
Keeping In Touch days to maintain my clinical skills in specialist areas, keep up to date with mandatory training,
and train in electronic systems introduced to our hospital. I used my pe

secarb__001_i.text:
rsonal time to maintain my research
knowledge and skills in order to prepare this Doctoral Clinical and Practitioner Academic Fellowship (DCAF)
application. I formed my supervisory team and met with them regularly throughout my maternity leave to prepare
my DCAF application. I have continued to engage with my PPI group and was awarded a Public Involvement Fund
grant for this PPI work (see ‘Research Grants held’ in Applicant CV section). I continued to attend research
network meetings and published my PCAF research outcomes in three separate manuscripts. I have continued to
develop my research and clinical networks, and have presented about the topic of feeding and swallowing in long-
term ventilation to various multidisciplinary teams across national and international forums. Despite being on
maternity leave, I have worked hard to develop my specialist clinical and research skills, and continue my
commitment to a career as a clinical academic.

secpesor__001_a.text:
Aims:
1. I will examine how services support ventilator-assisted children with feeding problems. I will collect
information from centres in the UK, New Zealand, Australia and Canada.
2. I will review patient records at three hospitals in England to:
a. identify the number of ventilator-assisted children with feeding problems
b. report the type of problems they had
3. I will assess ventilator-assisted children and interview their parents/carers three and six months after
hospital discharge to:
a. investigate the type of feeding problems children have
b. explore the psychological and social impacts of children’s feeding needs on their families
Background:
Ventilator-assisted children need long-term breathing support from a ventilation machine for some or all of the day
and night. Children receive ventilation through a face/nasal mask or tracheostomy (a surgically-created opening in
the neck). Their medical and ventilation needs may impact their ability to feed in a successful and safe way. In
2019, there were approximately 2383 ventilator-assisted children in the UK. This number is rising around the
world. Yet, there are no policies or guidelines about feeding support for these child
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
ren. There is little research
about the number of children with feeding problems and the types of difficulties they have. There is also a large
gap in exploring the psychological and social impacts of these issues on families. It is important to investigate
these topics so we can understand the needs of these children and their families.
Design and methods:
Project 1: Feeding services in the UK, Canada, Australia and New Zealand
I will collect information from online resources about service policies, guidelines and protocols. I will speak with
ventilation centres in these countries and invite health professionals working in hospital and community teams to
complete an online survey. This survey will include questions about how health professionals support ventilator-
assisted children with face/nasal mask and tracheostomy ventilation who have feeding problems.
Project 2: The number of children with feeding problems and the type of problems present
I will review the hospital notes of ventilator-assisted children under 5 years of age with face/nasal mask and
tracheostomy ventilation between 2021 to 2024. I will collect information from Evelina London Children’s Hospital,
Great Ormond
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
Street Hospital and Alder Hey Hospital. I will identify the number of children with feeding problems.
I will report on the problems they had at discharge and for 12 months after discharge from hospital.
Project 3: The types of feeding problems children have and the impacts on families
I will assess ventilator-assisted children under 5 years of age with face/nasal mask and tracheostomy ventilation
who are discharged from hospital needing feeding support. Parents/carers will complete questionnaires about their
child's feeding and its impacts. I will assess children’s eating and drinking in their home. I will interview
parents/carers to explore the psychological and social impacts of their children’s feeding on their families. If
children have a swallow x-ray study at the hospital, I will re-analyse these recordings.
Public involvement and engagement:
Parents of ventilator-assisted children and health professionals have contributed to my research proposal and this
plain English summary. I will meet with them and other expert health professionals to seek advice during the
projects. I will continue working with this group to plan and develop future research work.
Sharing research findin

gs:
My public involvement group and I will share research findings to a range of people and forums. We will speak at
conferences and meetings, and publish research findings in healthcare journals. I will use social media to provide
updates about my research to the public and relevant networks.

secsa__001_a.text:
Background: Ventilator-assisted children have underlying medical conditions that place them at risk of feeding
difficulties. In 2019, there were approximately 2383 ventilator-assisted children in the UK. This number is
anticipated to rise due to ongoing advancements in medical technology. However, there are no national policies or
guidelines about feeding support for this population. There is limited research about the prevalence and types of
feeding difficulties children present with. Moreso, the psychosocial impact of feeding difficulties on families has not
been thoroughly explored. Therefore, we have little understanding about the experiences and challenges faced by
children and their families. Their contributions are key in identifying future research priorities and addressing their
health and social care needs.
Overall research aim: To explore current practices/policies and investigate the occurrence, nature and
psychosocial impact of paediatric feeding disorders (PFDs) on ventilator-assisted children and their families.
Research objectives:
I will complete three studies investigating the needs of children receiving invasive and non-invasive long-term
ventilation:
1. Examine

secsa__001_b.text:
current national and international feeding service provision, policies and clinical practices.
2. Retrospectively identify the proportion of children under 5 years of age who presented with PFD at three
hospitals, and characterise the nature of their PFDs during and up to 12 months following hospital
discharge.
3. Prospectively assess and characterise the nature of PFDs of children under 5 years of age, and explore
the psychosocial impact of PFD on their families 3 and 6 months following hospital discharge.
Methods:
1) Environmental scan: I will synthesise evidence from online databases and grey literature about feeding
services, policies and protocols for ventilator-assisted children. I will gather information from stakeholders across
the UK, Canada, Australia and New Zealand. An online survey of health professionals in these countries will also
be conducted.
2) Retrospective cohort study: I will review the hospital records of ventilator-assisted children discharged from
Great Ormond Street Hospital, Evelina London Children’s Hospital and Alder Hey Hospital between 2021 to 2024.
I will identify the proportion of ventilator-assisted children with PFD and characterise feeding diffic

secsa__001_c.text:
ulties present at
discharge and up to 12 months following discharge.
3) Prospective case-series study: I will recruit ventilator-assisted children who are discharged from Great Ormond
Street Hospital and Evelina London Children’s Hospital with a referral for community feeding support. I will
characterise children’s PFD using parent questionnaires, video assessment and secondary analysis of
instrumental assessment. I will explore psychosocial impacts of feeding difficulties on families during interviews
with parents/caregivers.
Timelines for delivery:
 March 2026: Research regulatory processes
 August 2026: Environmental scan
 May 2027: Retrospective cohort study
April 2028: Prospective case-series study

 September 2028: PhD Thesis
Anticipated impact and dissemination:
This fellowship will meet research gaps in the fields of paediatric feeding and long-term ventilation. I will
disseminate findings through networks, relevant conferences and peer-reviewed journals. Research outcomes will
inform planning of research priorities for my post-doctoral work, including the development of evidence-based
practice guidelines and interventions to improve the feeding outcomes of children wi

secdrp__001_a.text:
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Introduction:
Ventilator-assisted children require long-term support from a ventilator to sustain or optimise their breathing6.
Ventilation is delivered non-invasively (via a face/nasal mask) or invasively (via a surgically-created opening in the
neck called a tracheostomy) for part or all of the day and night6. Ventilator-assisted children are a vulnerable group
of patients who have multiple long-term conditions/multimorbidity including respiratory difficulties, musculoskeletal
issues and/or central nervous system impairments7,8. In 2019, there were an estimated 2383 ventilator-assisted
children in the UK9. This number is anticipated to increase in the UK and globally due to advancements in medical
technology and earlier survival of premature babies6,9,10.
Children with paediatric feeding disorders (PFDs) present with impaired oral intake that is not typical for their age
and is associated with medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and/or psychosocial dysfunction11. These multi-faceted
problems significantly impact on daily life, restrict children from achieving age-appropriate milestones and limit
participation in normal childhood experience

secdrp__001_b.text:
s11. There are significant research gaps about a) PFDs and b) the
psychosocial impacts of PFDs on ventilator-assisted children and their families.
Problem 1: Research about paediatric feeding disorders in ventilator-assisted children is limited
A small body of research has found that ventilator-assisted children have poor feeding and swallowing outcomes
in the short and long term1,12–15. This research suggests that children are at high risk of dysphagia (swallowing
difficulty) and cannot swallow particular food and fluid textures safely1. They are often reliant on nasogastric or
gastrostomy feeding tubes to receive nutrition for some or all of their diet1. However, there are large research gaps
about the prevalence and nature of PFD in this population. Further research is required to understand their feeding
journey following initiation of ventilation and once discharged home from hospital. Investigating these issues is key
to understanding the true occurrence and impact of PFDs in ventilator-assisted children, and how best to devise
interventions to address these issues in the future.
Problem 2: There is limited research on the psychosocial impacts of paediatric feeding disorders

secdrp__001_c.text:
on ventilator-
assisted children and their families
PFDs impact many aspects of life for parents of children without ventilation, including higher anxiety, increased
stress and lower self-esteem16. Parents report that gastrostomy feeding is demanding, time-consuming and limits
their participation in everyday activities17. Parents in my qualitative research study discussed impacts on family
resources3.
bonding, social participation, time and family Ventilator-assisted families report a lower quality of life
than children with other chronic illnesses and the general population18. However, little research currently exists on
the psychosocial impacts of PFDs on ventilator-assisted children and their families. This is a significant research
gap that must be explored before we can identify ways to improve children’s feeding journeys.
2. Why is this research important?
Increasing knowledge and understanding about paediatric feeding disorders in children with complex health needs
is a current priority in the UK
A priority for global health research is to improve the lives of people with multiple long-term conditions/multi-
morbidity through research8,19. In 2022, The Royal College of Speec

secdrp__001_d.text:
h and Language Therapists identified the top
ten research priorities in dysphagia20. These included “what are the reported psychosocial effects of nasogastric
and gastrostomy feeding?” and “is feeding via gastrostomy effective in improving health and wellbeing outcomes
of children with neurodisability and dysphagia and their parents?”20. I will address these priorities in my research
proposal by exploring the psychosocial impact of PFDs on this population.
The feeding and swallowing problems of ventilator-assisted children place a significant economic burden on the
health system
Ventilator-assisted children have a significantly higher mortality, increased emergency department presentations
and longer hospital admissions than children with other chronic conditions21. PFDs can lead to adverse outcomes
including aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition, failure to thrive and dehydration11. The cost of staying on a long-term
ventilation and intensive care unit is £301,888 and £630,388 per year respectively22. The average cost of
maintaining a gastrostomy tube is £700 per year23. Appropriate management of PFDs is crucial to preventing
unnecessary economic burdens on the National Health Servic

secdrp__001_e.text:
e. By examining current service delivery and clinical
practices, as well as the frequency and severity of PFD, we will understand the true impact of feeding problems on
our health and social care systems.
3. Review of existing evidence
National and international services, policies and practices for ventilator-assisted children with paediatric feeding
disorders
Ventilator-assisted children receive care from large multidisciplinary teams24. In the UK, speech and language
therapists conduct assessments and treatments to improve safe feeding and swallowing skills25. Speech and
language therapists contribute vital information to the multidisciplinary team to allow for holistic patient-centred
decision-making24. However, there are no national clinical practice guidelines, service policies or protocols
regarding support for ventilator-assisted children with PFD. Therefore, we have limited knowledge about evidence-
based service delivery, centre policies and care practices. We also have little understanding about the feeding and
swallowing services that international centres provide. This data is required to inform the development of
interventions to improve the feeding journeys of these c

secdrp__001_f.text:
hildren and their families.
The nature of paediatric feeding disorders in ventilator-assisted children
Children who require short-term ventilation (for less than three months) experience dysphagia due to their
respiratory and co-morbid medical needs15. The presence of ventilation support and feeding tubes around the
mouth and face result in negative experiences that lead to chronic feeding difficulties such as oral aversion and
learnt refusal behaviours26–28. Research indicates that longer periods of ventilation result in worse feeding and
outcomes29.
swallowing
My scoping review investigated the feeding and swallowing outcomes of ventilator-assisted children1. 31 studies
were included in the scoping review. Only 5 studies contained more than 100 participants, 5 studies contained 50-
100 participants, and 21 studies contained less than 50 participants. Most studies investigated medical aspects of
ventilation (such as treatment outcomes and survival rates) and only 23% of studies focused on feeding outcomes
(feeding characteristics, tube feeding, assessment and intervention). Studies identified saliva management issues,
difficulty eating solids and drinking liquids, oral aversion an

secdrp__001_g.text:
d oral hypersensitivity. However, this research was
limited and non-specific in its reference to PFD. Outcomes were not described using consistent terminology and
were not characterised using the formal PFD framework. Reporting of assessment findings was not detailed e.g.,
did not describe the volumes and textures of food/drink tested, which affected the ability to draw evidence-based
conclusions. Therefore, research gaps continue to exist about the prevalence, severity and characteristics of PFD
experienced by this population.
The psychosocial impacts of paediatric feeding disorders on ventilator-assisted children and their families
Research about children without ventilation needs has shown that PFDs negatively affect the wellbeing and quality
of life of children and their families30,31. In my qualitative research study, parents reported similar impacts on
children, their siblings and other family members3. These discussions were insightful and revealed the potential
psychosocial issues that many ventilator-assisted children and their families experience. However, this study
contained a small self-selected sample of seven mothers. We need to conduct more in-depth research with a

secdrp__001_h.text:
larger sample of parents/caregivers from a diverse range of ethnic, gender, geographical and socioeconomic
backgrounds. This will ensure more accurate representation of the patient population and facilitate a holistic
understanding of the impact of PFD in this population.
4. Research Aims and Objectives
Overall research aim: To explore current practices and policies, as well as investigate the occurrence, nature and
psychosocial impact of PFDs on ventilator-assisted children and their families.
Research objectives:
1. Work package 1: To examine current national and international service provision, policies and clinical
practices used to support ventilator-assisted children with PFD.
2. Work package 2: To retrospectively identify the proportion of ventilator-assisted children under 5 years of
age who presented with PFD at three different children’s hospitals, and to characterise the nature of their
PFDs (across medical, nutritional, feeding skill and psychosocial domains) during and up to 12 months
following hospital discharge.
3. Work package 3: To prospectively assess and characterise the nature of PFDs (across medical,
nutritional, feeding skill and psychosocial domains) in venti

secdrp__001_i.text:
lator-assisted children under 5 years of age,
and explore the psychosocial impact of PFD on their families 3 and 6 months following hospital discharge.
5. Project Plan
I will conduct a series of studies employing quantitative and qualitative methodologies. I will form an expert
advisory group of health professionals and parents/caregivers to guide research planning, implementation and
dissemination. The outcomes of these projects will inform the development of tools to support families and
healthcare professionals in the management of these children in my post-doctoral work.
Work package 1: Environmental scan
I will complete an environmental scan examining current services, policies and practices that multidisciplinary
ventilation teams use to support PFDs in the UK, Australia, New Zealand and Canada. I have selected these
countries as they have similar healthcare systems to the UK (i.e., healthcare is publicly funded). This
environmental scan can inform future planning and implementation of national service delivery, policies and clinical
practices.
Design:
I will use the seven-step environmental scan approach proposed and used by Wilburn and colleagues, as it is
applicable to hea

secdrp__001_j.text:
lthcare and the objectives of this work package32:
1. Determine leadership and capacity for the project: Together with my expert advisory group, I will lead the
process from development to dissemination under the guidance of my supervision team.
2. Establish the focal area and purpose: The purpose is to collect and synthesise information on services,
policies and practices about feeding and swallowing management for ventilator-assisted children.
3. Create and adhere to a timeline and set incremental goals: I provide an overview of the timeline in my
Gantt chart and this will be further detailed during planning with my advisory group.
4. Determine information to be collected and 5. Identify and engage stakeholders:
1) I will conduct a review of peer-reviewed and grey literature including evidence reviews, guidelines,
policy documents, websites, letters/opinion pieces and research studies. I will search online databases
(PubMed, MEDLINE (Ovid Version), Cochrane library, Web of Science and CINAHL Plus) using a search
strategy created with a research librarian. Studies published from 1980 will be included as the research
field of long-term ventilation has significantly developed since

secdrp__001_k.text:
this time. I will screen studies according to
the eligibility criteria below. Relevant citations will be uploaded into Covidence and extracted using a
bespoke extraction tool. I will search for grey literature using handsearching via a generic search engine in
Google.
Eligibility criteria:
 Population: children aged 0-5 years
 Subject: long-term ventilation (invasive and non-invasive ventilation provided for at least 3 months from
time of initiation and once medically stable)
 Context: feeding and swallowing
2) I will seek relevant stakeholders (e.g. respiratory teams at Great Ormond Street Hospital in the UK, The
Hospital for Sick Children in Canada, Sydney Children’s Hospital in Australia and the Starship Hospital in
New Zealand) through professional societies, my fellowship and health networks, and professional
networks of my supervisors/expert advisory panel. I will communicate with a key contact from each
stakeholder group through email. I anticipate a sample of 20-30 stakeholder groups based on engagement
with at least 5 service providers in each country. I will obtain information/documentation about service
delivery, team members, policies, protocols and practices. Inform

secdrp__001_l.text:
ation will be entered into the bespoke
extraction tool used in step 1.
3: I will design and distribute an online survey. The online survey will examine clinical practice,
assessment and intervention tools, barriers and facilitators to service provision, and research priorities.
Survey development will be informed by literature review and stakeholder engagement processes.
Previous/existing surveys in this topic will also inform survey design. I will meet with my advisory group
and supervisory team to reach consensus about final item selection. The survey will be pilot tested for
content validity using purposive sampling of 5 hospital sites in the UK. The survey will be conducted on the
Qualtrics website.
Health professionals in acute and community settings in all four countries will be invited to participate.
Ventilations teams typically consist of a respiratory consultant, specialist nurse, physiotherapist,
occupational therapist, psychologist, speech and language therapist and dietician6. I anticipate
participation from at least 5 ventilation teams in each country. If international centres have similar teams
as the UK, I predict a sample of 100 participants.
6. Analyse and synthes

secdrp__001_m.text:
ise results into summary report:
I will analyse data using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) and triangulate data according
to the environmental scan. I will conduct a gap analysis to identify missing information.
Online survey responses will be analysed using SPSS software. I will use descriptive statistics (counts and
percentages) to analyse quantitative findings. I will conduct summative content analysis of qualitative
responses to open-ended questions. I will code qualitative responses, check codes with the supervisory
team then quantify codes across the data. The Checklist for Reporting Results of Internet E-Surveys will
be used to report survey data33.
7. Disseminate results and conclusions to key stakeholders: See ‘dissemination, outputs and anticipated
impacts’ below.
Work package 2: Retrospective cohort study
Design:
I will conduct a retrospective observational cohort study using data from medical records of ventilator-assisted
patients from Great Ormond Street Hospital, Evelina London Children’s Hospital and Alder Hey Hospital between
the years of 2021 to 2024. A retrospective study will allow me to obtain data about the occurrence of feeding
problems (in

secdrp__001_n.text:
these hospitals) and report on the types of problems experienced. These hospitals are specialist
tertiary centres with large catchment areas across London, and northwest and southeast England. Participants will
therefore represent a diverse range of demographic, social and ethnic backgrounds.
I will collect data from 2021 given the integrity of electronic medical records from this date onwards. I will review
inpatient and outpatient medical records, including those documented at time of discharge and for 12 months
following discharge.
I will include data from children aged 0 to 5 years as this is a critical period of development for feeding skills34.
There are approximately 20 children initiated on long-term ventilation (aged 0–5 years) at each site per year (as
per hospital databases). Therefore, I anticipate a sample of approximately 180 children.
Inclusion criteria:
 Children aged 0–5 years discharged following initiation of invasive or non-invasive long-term ventilation
between 2021 to 2024
Exclusion criteria:
 Children in whom ventilation was not long-term (discontinued within three months from initiation and once
medically stable)
Data collection and analysis:
1. I will sc

secdrp__001_o.text:
reen patient records from September 2021 to September 2024 and collect data on demographics
and medical history.
2. I will identify the proportion of ventilator-assisted patients with PFD using diagnostic criteria proposed by
Goday and colleagues: disturbance in oral intake of nutrients, inappropriate for age, lasting at least 2
weeks and associated with one or more of the following: medical, nutritional, feeding skill and/or
psychosocial dysfunction11.
3. I will extract data on the characteristics of PFD at hospital discharge and for 12 months after discharge to
populate the following:
 PFD Case Report Form (CRF)35 – this form characterises PFD across medical, nutritional, feeding skill
and psychosocial domains.
 Rosenbek Penetration-Aspiration Scale (PAS)36 – a rank ordered scale from 1 to 8 that classifies depth of
food/drink entering the airway in videofluoroscopic swallow studies. I will score the PAS based on
videofluoroscopic swallow study reports (if information is available).
(CEDAS)37
 Consensus on Level Descriptors for a Functional Children’s Eating and Drinking Activity Scale
– a scale from 1 to 6 that describes a child’s oral/non-oral feeding status. I will report t

secdrp__001_p.text:
he score on
discharge and 12 months after discharge.
4. I will record and code data in a password-protected Excel spreadsheet then transfer it into SPSS for
analyses. All demographic, medical history and assessment information will be presented using
descriptive statistics. Categorical data will be presented as counts and percentages. Continuous data will
be presented as means and standard deviations or medians and interquartile ranges depending on data
distribution.
Work package 3: Prospective case series study
I will conduct a prospective mixed-methods case series study. This will allow me to collect data that will inform my
research objectives with a feasible sample of patients and their families within the fellowship timeframes.
I will invite patients and their parents/caregivers at Evelina London Children’s Hospital and Great Ormond Street
Hospital who are being discharged home with a referral for ongoing feeding support. I will recruit families between
March 2026 and March 2027.
Inclusion criteria:
 Children aged 0 – 5 years being discharged following initiation of invasive or non-invasive long-term
ventilation with a referral to a community speech and language therapist for

secdrp__001_q.text:
feeding support
 Families who pass the screening risk assessment for a home visit (e.g., no active safeguarding
investigation, no known domestic violence situation)
 Families living in England
Approximately 15 ventilator-assisted children who meet above inclusion criteria are discharged from each site per
year (as per hospital databases). Allowing for recruitment of 60-70% of eligible patients, I anticipate a sample of
18-21 children and their parents/caregivers.
Data collection:
Children will be recruited prior to discharge from hospital and followed up at 3 and 6 months after discharge. I will
complete the following steps:
1. I will collaborate with respiratory and speech and language therapy teams to screen children who meet the
inclusion criteria. The clinical team will inform parents/caregivers about the study. If they express interest, I
will meet with them in the hospital to explain the study and provide information sheets/consent forms.
Parents/caregivers will be given at least 24 hours to consent and return forms. Translation of research
participant material into other languages will be completed as needed.
2. I will access participants’ medical records and collect data

secdrp__001_r.text:
about demographics and medical history.
3. I will schedule three sessions with the family:
a. 3 months post-discharge: an online one-to-one interview with parents/caregivers
b. 6 months post-discharge: a home visit
c. 2 weeks after the home visit: an online one-to-one interview with parents/caregivers
4. Three months after discharge, I will conduct qualitative parent/caregiver interviews over
Zoom/Teams/phone call (depending on their preference) to allow for convenience and flexibility as per
families’ needs. I will use a topic guide informed by the evidence base and developed with my expert
advisory group. This will include questions about the impact of the child’s PFD on their family, daily
activities, emotional functioning, social functioning and quality of life. Open-ended questions will be used to
encourage participant-led discussion. I anticipate interviews to run for 60 minutes. Online interviews will be
recorded and transcribed using Zoom/Teams software. Phone calls will be recorded and transcribed by
myself. Interpreting services will be used.
5. One week prior to the home visit, I will send families two parent questionnaires to complete and return by
email. If families d

secdrp__001_s.text:
on’t have email, they will complete questionnaires during the home visit.
 The Paediatric Eating Assessment Tool (Pedi-EAT)38 – a questionnaire used to assess characteristics of
PFD. Parent/caregivers rate the frequency of physiological symptoms, problematic mealtime behaviours,
selective/restrictive eating and oral processing difficulties.
(FS-IS)39
 The Feeding/Swallowing Impact Survey – a questionnaire used to measure psychosocial
impacts of children’s feeding on their parent/caregiver. Parent/caregivers rate the frequency of impacts on
daily activities, worrying and feeding.
6. During the home visit, I will conduct an assessment of the child’s feeding and swallowing skills by
recording the child eating/drinking during a mealtime.
After each home visit, I will analyse the video and characterise children's PFD using:
 PFD CRF35 – described in work package 2
 CEDAS37 – described in work package 2
7. I will conduct a second qualitative interview with each parent/caregiver at 6 months post-discharge. This
interview will use the same process as step 4 and aim to obtain a review of their views and experiences on
the same questions and topics.
8. If a child receives a videofluorosc

secdrp__001_t.text:
opic swallow study during the study period as part of the management
plan prescribed by their community speech and language therapist, I will conduct secondary analysis of
study recordings. A videofluoroscopic swallow study is an instrumental assessment conducted by speech
and language therapists and radiographers in an x-ray room. Children eat/drink while moving images of
their swallowing are recorded and evaluated by the speech and language therapy team40. This is typically
conducted at the child’s tertiary hospital (i.e., Great Ormond Street Hospital/Evelina London Children’s
Hospital).
I will access videofluoroscopic swallow study recordings from hospital databases and analyse them using:
 Rosenbek PAS – described in work package 2
 BaByVFSS Impairment Profile (BaByVFSSImP) or Modified Barium Study Impairment Profile
(MBSImP)36,41,42 - standardised analysis and reporting tools for videofluoroscopic swallow studies. The age
of the patient/type of feeding assessed determines which tool to use.
Feeding and swallowing measurement tools used:
A summary of the tools that will be used to characterise PFDs are listed in Table 1. They provide information
across the four domains of PFD

.

Data analysis:
1. I will analyse feeding and swallowing assessment data, including frame-by-frame secondary analysis of
videofluoroscopic swallow study recordings. I will score parent-reported questionnaires according to
assessment scoring instructions. I will input assessment data into a password-protected Excel
spreadsheet then transfer into SPSS. Data analysis will follow the process described in work package 2.
2. Qualitative parent interviews will be transcribed using Zoom/Teams software (and I will transcribe phone
calls). I will check the accuracy of transcripts by re-watching/re-listening to interviews. Interviews will be
anonymised and pseudonyms will be used. I will conduct reflexive thematic analysis to analyse interviews
using Braun and Clarke’s approach43. This includes familiarisation with the data, generating initial codes,
generating themes, reviewing potential themes, defining and naming themes, and producing a report43. A
proportion of intervie

secdrp__001_v.text:
ws will be re-coded by a second rater for trustworthiness. I will have a consensus
discussion with my supervisors regarding final themes. Results will be shared with the advisory group for
sense-checking. Participants will have an opportunity to review findings and ensure interpretation is
accurate.
Synthesis of work packages:
By completing all three work packages, I will address the research gaps in understanding the occurrence, nature
and impact of PFD in ventilator-assisted children under 5 years of age. These findings, combined with the
environmental scan, will provide a holistic picture of children’s feeding journeys during and following hospital
discharge. This will provide an evidence-based foundation for identifying future research priorities, including i) the
development of national guidelines regarding service delivery and practice, and ii) design of intervention tools to
improve feeding outcomes of children with long-term ventilation needs.
6. Dissemination, Outputs, and Anticipated Impacts
Together with my supervisory team and expert advisory group, I will co-develop accessible outputs for
dissemination. I will write a lay summary to stakeholders (e.g. charities, servic

secdrp__001_w.text:
e providers, respiratory and speech
and language therapy teams at hospital sites) and study participants. I will share findings through research groups,
professional associations and ventilation centres. I will publish in open-access peer-reviewed journals and
disseminate outcomes at conferences (see conferences in ‘training and development plan’). These activities will
be completed with my PPI group. I will create a Twitter/X account to raise awareness of my study and post
updates about recruitment, dissemination and outputs. With my advisory group/supervisory team, I will write a
best-practice statement regarding service delivery and clinical practice, which will be further developed in my post-
doctoral research work.
Potential barriers for future research, development, adoption and implementation:
Work package 1:
 Some grey literature/documents may be subject to intellectual property protection - I will reference
appropriately when disseminating findings.
 This work package involves developed countries only - findings will only be applicable to nations with
similar health economies.
 Cultural considerations in each country may affect their adoption/implementation of research

secdrp__001_x.text:
outcomes in
similar ways – I will acknowledge this in planning, data analysis and dissemination stages.
Work package 2:
 Multicentre studies can be challenging, particularly at sites that are not my main work site - I will obtain a
research passport to conduct research across sites. I will adhere to good clinical practice guidelines at all
times.
 Other potential barriers include missing/incomplete patient records, recall bias, observer bias and
technology issues - these will be mitigated by adhering to a rigorous and prospective missing data
handling protocol.
Work package 3:
 Potential concerns about data security - the study will be registered with King’s College London (KCL) data
protection team. The collection, handling and storage of study data will follow KCL data protection
guidelines.
 Generalisation of results to the wider population of ventilator-assisted children will be difficult due to the
small sample size – this will be acknowledged in data analysis and dissemination stages.
Anticipated impacts:
Research findings will inform current practice of long-term ventilation teams across the UK and has potential to
influence future service delivery and healthcare. Outco

secdrp__001_y.text:
mes from the environmental scan will provide an overview
of local and international centres which will promote discussion about evidence-based care. Findings from work
packages two and three will meet current research gaps and provide necessary knowledge to clinicians and
academics working in the fields of respiratory medicine and paediatric feeding. These impacts are anticipated to
reach service users, the public and services during the fellowship and continue benefitting health and social care
sectors after the fellowship.
7. Project Management
With guidance from my supervisory team, I will be responsible for managing the timeline and commitments in the
fellowship. This includes completing management and governance activities, work packages, training/development
and professional development. I will complete the KCL course ‘Completing your PhD on time, on budget, and
under control’ to learn effective time management and project management skills.
I will meet with my primary supervisor every 2 weeks and with the full supervision team monthly. I will attend Guy’s
and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust and King’s Health Partners doctoral peer support groups. I will continue to
utilise t

secdrp__001_z.text:
he NIHR Research Support Service for support with research planning, implementation and dissemination.
The primary tasks I will be responsible for (with guidance from my supervisors):
 Ethics submission and data protection registration
 Organising expert advisory group meetings
 Dissemination of study recruitment information
 Recruitment of participants
 Conducting NIHR Equality Impact Assessments to ensure equality, diversity and inclusion for study
participants44
 Purchasing project equipment
 Planning/development/completion of work packages
 Study data collection and analysis
 Feedback to stakeholders/participants
 Completing my training plan
 Collaboration with PPI groups
 Networking with fellowship collaborators
 Organising attendance at conferences/workshops/presentations
 Managing fellowship research budget (see ‘detailed budget’ section)
 Disseminating research findings
 Completing PhD thesis
8. Ethics
I will abide by my professional body’s and KCL’s code of conduct. I will obtain ethical approval from the Health
Research Authority, KCL and Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust. I will complete data registration with
KCL’s data protection office. Study p

secdrp__001_{.text:
rocedures will follow ethical guidelines concerning confidentiality, consent,
data maintenance and data storage.
Potential ethical issues:
 Clinical assessments will be conducted in families’ homes in work package 3. I will follow the
university’s lone working policy. I will screen out high-risk visits as part of the exclusion criteria.
Prior to visiting homes, I will call families to conduct a visit risk assessment (e.g. identify who will
be present, animals on site, parking/access from public transport, environmental dangers)). During
visits, I will share my location with my supervisors and check in with my supervisors before/after
visits using ‘My SOS’ mobile phone software.
 In the event of a child experiencing clinical deterioration during a home visit, I will use an
established hospital trust Standard Operating Procedure to escalate this to appropriate clinical
services.
There are no anticipated risks to families during home visits outside of typical speech and

language therapy practice. I have a current Disclosure and Barring Service check and have
completed mandatory health and safeguarding training.
 Qualitative interviews with parents/caregivers may result in discuss

secdrp__001_|.text:
ion of sensitive/distressing
issues. If participants become upset, I will offer them a break and opportunity to reschedule the
interview. Information sheets/consent forms will contain full disclosure about participants’ right to
withdraw at any time. I will remind distressed participants they can withdraw from the study and
signpost them to appropriate self-referral mental health support as needed.
 Recordings from home visits and interviews will be transferred onto an encrypted hard drive and
deleted from the camcorder memory card/Zoom/Teams software within 24 hours of being
recorded. Analysis will be completed in a confidential space and will not be shared during
dissemination. Information sheets/consent forms will outline processes about confidentiality and
recording.
 I am a highly specialist speech and language therapist and will uphold boundaries between my
clinical and research roles. During work package 3, I may identify a clinical need for review from
the child’s community speech and language therapist (e.g. reported recent change in feeding
skills). As all participants will have community feeding support, I will signpost the family to discuss
concerns with their communi

secdrp__001_}.text:
ty team.
I will ensure there is no conflict of interest when planning, completing and disseminating research

studies in the fellowship.
9. Success Criteria
Key risks:
 Delays in ethical approval - I will ensure ethical approval is a high priority and allow at least 6 months for
approval.
 Delays in completing work packages - the research timetable accounts for leeway in case of unforeseen
circumstances.
 Low response rate from stakeholders in work package 1 - my supervisory team have pre-existing research
relationships with centres in included countries. I will maximise efforts to use my fellowship collaboration
groups/research networks.
 Poor uptake of recruitment in work package 3 - recruitment efforts will be optimised using the NIHR Be
Part of Research registry.
 Rescheduling assessments in work package 3 - families may need to reschedule sessions due to
illness/hospitalisations/medical appointments. I will allow for flexibility/leeway in the project timeline. If data
is collected outside the 3- and 6-month period, reasons will be stated when reporting findings.
 Attrition of participants in work package 3 - I will conduct interviews at two timepoints to facilitate ongo

secdrp__001_~.text:
ing
engagement. Conducting initial interviews at 3 months post-discharge also accounts for potential
attrition/inability to collect this data at 6 months post-discharge.
Success criteria:
 Work package 1: Engagement with at least 10 centres; participation from at least 20 health professionals
in each country in online survey.
 Work package 2: Accessibility to data at each site.
 Work package 3: Recruitment of at least 10 families.

secppi__001_a.text:
Please describe how patients/service users, carers and the public have been involved in developing this
proposal
I used funding awarded from the NIHR Research Design Service Public Involvement Fund to seek feedback about
my research proposal from 3 parents of ventilator-assisted children and 3 health professionals. They also provided
feedback on my plain English summary.
Research aims: All agreed that the research proposal is valuable and will make a positive impact on children and
families. They stated that investigating children’s needs after hospital discharge is important and often forgotten in
research.
Methods:
 Work package 1: Parents stated the importance of engaging with a range of health professionals who
represent both acute and community services.
 Work package 2: The group reported that all three hospitals were suitable sites to collect data due to their
large catchment areas and the inclusion of children from a diverse range of demographic, social and
cultural backgrounds.
 Work package 3: Parents and health professionals stated that assessing children’s PFD characteristics
was “needed”. They agreed with the clinical assessments chosen. They felt that the video ass

secppi__001_b.text:
essment
should be conducted in the child’s home to be most representative of their eating and drinking skills. They
agreed that reporting on videofluoroscopic swallow study results was important. Parents felt conducting a
home visit will allow parents/caregivers to build rapport with me and increase the likelihood of them
engaging in an open manner in the interviews. Both parents and health professionals felt that exploring the
psychosocial impacts on families was “very important” and a valuable part of my research proposal.
 This group felt that the three work packages complemented each other and will provide holistic information
about children’s feeding journeys and the impacts on their family’s lives.
Important considerations:
 Work package 1: Health professionals stated that research should be considerate of the cultural history in
different countries, such as respecting first nations groups and acknowledging social health disparities.
 Work package 2: Health professionals discussed potential challenges regarding missing/incomplete
patient data.
 Work package 3: Parents emphasised the need to consider the daily stresses of families’ lives. Health
professionals agreed that m

secppi__001_c.text:
ethods should involve a large degree of flexibility and convenience for families.
Please describe the ways in which patients/service users, carers and the public will be actively involved in
the proposed research, including any training and support provided
Following the completion of my PCAF, I have maintained a strong research relationship with parents from the
qualitative research study. Three parents have expressed interest in being actively involved in ongoing research
activities. These parents will be invited to join an expert advisory group. I will advertise PPI opportunities on the
NIHR People in Research website to widen access and participation for greater diversity and inclusion. If access to
technology is a barrier for members, face-to-face meetings will take place. Support for individuals with hearing,
mobility and visual impairments will be provided. Language translation/interpretation services will be provided.
The expert advisory group will also consist of health professionals working with ventilator-assisted children. I will
meet with the advisory group every three months. They will guide wording of study materials (e.g., ethics
application, participant consent and

secppi__001_d.text:
information forms) and assist with dissemination of recruitment details and
research findings. Parents will be compensated for their time and contribution as per NIHR payment guidelines45.
I have attended PPI training and will continue to develop my skills and knowledge in this area. I will seek an
experienced PPI mentor from my PPI group to guide planning of PPI activities and seek advice about PPI
engagement/withdrawal. I will choose a mentor from my PPI group as these parents are regularly involved in
research for WellChild charity and have experience in contributing to research/PPI activities about long-term
ventilation.
PPI members will be invited to contribute to dissemination of research findings throughout the fellowship. This will
include involvement in patient engagement activities, joint presentation of findings at conferences/workshops, and
co-writing of articles in relevant publications (e.g. open-access journal articles, newsletters, magazine articles). I
will provide information and compensation for relevant training courses that the PPI group may like to attend, such
as training for public speaking and writing courses.
I will engage with relevant patient groups and

secppi__001_e.text:
the public during patient engagement events to disseminate project
information. I will also raise awareness about my research studies through social media, patient forums and charity
networks.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve patients/service users, carers and the
public in your proposed research, please justify why
Not applicable.

sectdars__001_a.text:
Proposed training and development programme
I will enrol in a PhD at KCL under the Applied Technologies for Clinical Care research division in the Florence
Nightingale Faculty of Nursing, Midwifery and Palliative Care (NMPC). I have used the Vitae Researcher
Developmental Framework to prepare and structure my plan. Please see my Gantt chart for anticipated time
commitments of each course/workshop.
Formal study: I will access courses from the KCL Centre for Doctoral Studies and Health Sciences Doctoral
Training Centre which are ideal environments to develop my personal, professional and career development goals.
These centres have confirmed that most courses run for 1-2 hours.
Year 1: 1) Research integrity, 2) Collaboration essentials to solve complex problems, 3) Advanced quantitative
research methods, 4) Completing your PhD on time, on budget, and under control
Year 2: 1) Managing research data, 2) Advanced qualitative research methods, 3) Qualitative interviewing
Year 3: 1) Writing up the thesis in the sciences, 2) Presenting your research with confidence, 3) Health
professional researchers: managing dual careers webinars
Additional training in researcher and academic leader skil

sectdars__001_d.text:
a research visit to The Hospital for Sick Children in Toronto. This hospital is
Canada’s most research-intensive paediatric hospital and the largest paediatric tertiary centre in the country. Their
multidisciplinary complex respiratory care clinic supports children with multiple long-term conditions who have
ventilation support at home. Their International Learner Program involves observation experiences. I will learn
about their model of patient care for ventilator-assisted children. I will share my research outcomes and develop
networks with clinical and research teams. I will also explore their research endeavours and foster opportunities for
future collaboration.
Professional practice:
I will dedicate 20% of my fellowship to professional practice to maintain clinical competencies and develop skills to
become an expert clinical leader in my research area.
I will continue to work at Evelina London Children’s Hospital either once a fortnight/month depending on other
fellowship commitments at the time. I will maintain required competencies in acute paediatric dysphagia and
continue to develop my specialist clinical skills in respiratory medicine (especially long-term ventilation ca
sectdars__001_d.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_e.text:
re). I will
progress my leadership skills by taking on supervision and mentoring roles within my team.
In order to advance my specialist clinical skills in paediatric ventilation, I will complete a bespoke virtual education
and mentorship program from The Hospital for Sick Children in Toronto as part of their International Learner
Program. A faculty member will regularly meet with me throughout my fellowship to explore issues in patient care
and practice, and create a bespoke online learning program containing relevant modules/courses. Remote
mentoring will be provided by a specialist respiratory consultant.
In my PCAF, I met with respiratory consultants across the South Thames region of London and observed their
multidisciplinary long-term ventilation clinics. In the DCAF, I will complete similar activities across hospitals in the
North Thames region of London on a bimonthly basis. This has been agreed with the Pan-Thames Long-Term
Ventilation Programme. This will advance my specialist clinical skills and raise my profile as a clinical academic
across these health networks.
I will dedicate the remainder of professional practice time to attending associated professional development

sectdars__001_f.text:
. This
will include training for BaByVFSSImP, a new standardised assessment tool for interpreting and reporting
videofluoroscopic swallow study results. This will provide me with required training for this tool in work package 3. I
will also attend a Fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing (FEES) training course. FEES is a useful
instrumental assessment for evaluating swallowing in ventilator-assisted children. Completing this training will
allow me to improve my paediatric feeding skills and specialist knowledge in this area.
Conferences:
I will maintain up-to-date knowledge of emerging research, disseminate research findings and build networks
during these conferences. These events will also raise my profile as a clinical academic in my field.
 UK Swallow Research Group (2026, 2028)
 Home Mechanical Ventilation UK Meeting (2026)
 European Society of Swallowing Disorders (2027)
 Feeding Matters conference (2027)
 Royal College of Speech and Language Therapy Conference (2027)

Name Professor Louise Rose
