sectdars__001_g.text:
Midwifery and Palliative Care
Description of support provided
My primary supervisor is Professor Rose who is a Professor of Critical Care Nursing and Research Division Head
at the Florence Nightingale Faculty of Nursing, Midwifery and Palliative Care at King’s College London. She also
holds an honorary Professorship in the Department of Critical Care and at the Lane Fox Respiratory Unit, St.
Thomas’s Hospital. Professor Rose has authored over 240 peer-reviewed publications, seven book chapters and
is a Cochrane Collaboration systematic review author. Her H-index is 55, i10 index is 139, with 9062 citations
(April 2024). She has been awarded 114 peer reviewed competitive research grants totalling £20,275,083 with
£7,494,884 as chief/primary investigator.
Professor Rose has research interests that include the trajectory of mechanically ventilated patients from home to
intensive care. She has substantial experience in mixed-methods, randomised controlled trial study designs,
process evaluations and qualitative interviews.
Professor Rose is currently supervising seven PhD students, three as primary supervisor (all funded NIHR) and
four as supervisory committee member at various univer
sectdars__001_g.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_h.text:
sities in the UK and internationally. She is also
supervising one NIHR Pre-doctoral Clinical Academic Fellow (PCAF), two Masters students, and one NIHR
Advanced Clinical Academic Fellowship. She has supervised to completion a further six PhD students, three post-
doctoral fellows, five PCAFs and two UK Masters Dissertation students.
Professor Rose has 90% dedicated time to research and academic supervision and has ample availability to
provide supervision of an exemplary standard. She has provided me with valuable support so far and she is an
ideal primary supervisor for my fellowship.
Additional Academic Supervisor(s)
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 1
Name Professor Jo Wray
Institution University College London
Position Senior Research Fellow and Health Psychologist
Description of support provided
Dr Jo Wray is an experienced health psychologist and leads a programme of mixed-methods psychosocial
research in the Heart and Lung directorate at Great Ormond Street Children’s Hospital (GOSH). She is a
Professor of Child Health Psychology in the Institute of Cardiovascular Science at UCL (Cardiology, Critical Care
and Transplantation) and a key element of her GOSH and institutional role
sectdars__001_h.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_i.text:
is to support and enable clinical
academics from a range of nursing and allied health professional backgrounds, as well as develop, mentor and
supervise early career researchers. She currently supervises six PhD students from a range of clinical
backgrounds, four of whom are NIHR doctoral fellows (she is primary supervisor for two, both are exploring the
impact of feeding disorders associated with chronic conditions). Previously she supervised eight PhD students,
four doctoral clinical psychology students and eight Masters students to completion.
Although Dr Wray works in a different institution to the applicant, provision of regular supervision will be
accommodated as she has protected time to provide academic supervision. The proposed project fits well with Dr
Wray’s current research programme due to her focus on, and interest in, mixed-methods research, particularly with
children with complex conditions and their families. She has considerable experience of working with children and
families using a wide range of methodologies and is currently a co-applicant on several NIHR-funded projects
which include qualitative elements exploring patient and family experiences of illness an
sectdars__001_i.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_j.text:
d impact of treatment,
quantitative elements related to clinical and psychosocial data and evidence synthesis.
ADDITIONAL ACADEMIC SUPERVISOR 2
Name Dr Jeanne Marshall
Institution Children's Health Queensland and The University of Queensland
Position Conjoint Senior Research Fellow
Description of support provided
Dr Jeanne Marshall is a senior conjoint research fellow at Queensland Children’s Hospital and the University of
Queensland. Dr Marshall has an expanding research portfolio in the area of paediatric feeding disorder, as well as
significant clinical experience as a speech pathologist at Queensland Children’s Hospital. Dr Marshall has a career
total of 36 publications, 252 citations, a h-index of 10, and a current field-weighted citation index of 2.04 (Scopus,
May 2024). Dr Marshall has gained funding for her work via individually-led grant applications, and through
mentorship of clinicians and students (career total $395,224). Additionally, Dr Marshall is a consortium
representative for Children’s Health Queensland on a $6.9 million national project. Dr Marshall has
provided/currently provides supervision to 9 research higher degree students. Four of these students have
grad
sectdars__001_j.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_k.text:
uated (1x PhD, 2x Masters, 1x clinical doctorate), and 5 students are current PhD students. Supervision has
been provided both in Australia, and internationally in the UK and the United States. Dr Marshall has a dedicated
80% of time to research activity, and has availability to support supervision of this project.
Dr Marshall will provide expertise on paediatric feeding and dysphagia in clinical research. She was my clinical
academic mentor and a valuable part of my supervision team during my PCAF. Her contribution to my PCAF has
been integral to the publishing and dissemination of our research outcomes. She has a passion for research
translation and building research capacity, and is an inspiration for clinical academic speech and language
therapists.
Clinical Supervisor(s)
PRACTICE SUPERVISOR 1
Name Alexandra Stewart
Institution Great Ormond Street Hospital
Position Speech and Language Therapist and current DCAF Fellow
Description of support provided
Alex has been working as a speech and language therapist in the field of paediatric feeding difficulties for over 20
years. She is based at Great Ormond Street Hospital and is due to complete an NIHR-funded Clinical Doctoral
Researc
sectdars__001_k.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_l.text:
h Fellowship in July 2024. She has provided clinical mentorship to three speech and language therapists
undertaking PCAFs (2 completed, 1 current) and supervised six MSc research projects to completion. Alex is
chair of the Royal College of Speech and Language Therapists paediatric dysphagia clinical excellence network,
scientific chair of the UK Swallowing Research Group, has been involved in a number of professional body
working groups and founded the Paediatric Dysphagia Speech and Language Therapists in Research network.
She has experience of leading and innovating within clinical practice. She is currently establishing use of high-
resolution impedance manometry in pharyngeal swallow assessment in the UK. Alex will provide supervision once
every two months which will include development of clinical skills, such as use of fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation
of swallowing, support with clinical aspects of the research project and mentorship to implement changes in clinical
practice. In addition, she is extremely well-placed to strengthen access to a network of researchers and clinicians
in the field, provide guidance on managing competing aspects of the fellowship and champion my d
sectdars__001_l.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_m.text:
evelopment as
a clinical academic.
Collaborations
WellChild Charity: WellChild is a national charity for children with complex health needs who are cared for at
home. I collaborated with WellChild in my PCAF and have a strong clinical academic relationship with their team. I
will have access to their family engagement team, family network and multidisciplinary team who are specialised in
long-term ventilation. WellChild charity has agreed to share research study information on social media and
disseminate research findings. I will plan ongoing opportunities for networking and research endeavours for
collaborations in future post-doctoral work.
Pan-Thames Paediatric Long-Term Ventilation Programme: This programme is commissioned by NHS
England London and geographically covers both the South and North Thames Paediatric Operational Delivery
Networks (ODNs). I collaborated with the South Thames Long-Term Ventilation Paediatric Network in my PCAF
and have a strong clinical academic relationship with their team. The Pan-Thames programme has agreed to
share research study information through their network, recruit participants and disseminate research findings. I
will visit respiratory cl
sectdars__001_m.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_n.text:
inics that are part of the North Thames Paediatric ODNs to advance my specialist knowledge.
Working in collaboration with this team provides me with a robust group of health professionals and managers to
collaborate with in current and future research endeavours.
Acute paediatric speech and language therapy teams at Evelina London Children’s Hospital and Great Ormond
Street Hospital: These two teams provide specialist tertiary care to ventilator-assisted children with feeding and
swallowing needs. I will collaborate with them during completion of work package three to screen for eligible
participants. I will also disseminate my research findings to these teams and their networks.
Respiratory teams at Evelina London Children’s Hospital and Great Ormond Street Hospital: These two large
specialist tertiary teams contain respiratory consultants, specialist nurses and allied health professionals.
Collaboration with these teams allows me to share research study information, recruit participants and disseminate
findings through their networks. I will raise my profile as a clinical academic across these hospitals and their wider
health networks in England.
Multidisciplinary Complex Respira
sectdars__001_n.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_o.text:
tory Care Clinic at The Hospital For Sick Children in Toronto: This hospital’s
mission statement involves partnering globally to improve children’s health outcomes in care, research and
education. I will complete an overseas research visit as part of the hospital’s International Learner Program which
involves observational experiences and a virtual education/mentoring program. This will advance my clinical and
research skills in this area. I anticipate this collaboration leading to ongoing networking, raising of my profile as a
clinical academic, and future opportunities for international research.
Expert Advisory Group: This group will involve the following health professionals whom have agreed to collaborate
with me during this fellowship –
 Alison Felton (Highly specialist speech and language therapist): Alison works at Alder Hey Hospital and
specialises in the assessment and management of neuromuscular conditions and long-term ventilation.
She completed an NIHR PCAF in 2023 and focused on the topic of spinal muscular atrophy during this
fellowship. Her knowledge and skills in the field of ventilation care in both clinical and research fields will
be integral to the planning an
sectdars__001_o.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_p.text:
d implementation of my research proposal.
 Respiratory consultants (Dr Jane Heraghty and Dr Simona Turcu): Dr Heraghty and Dr Turcu are
paediatric respiratory consultants at Evelina London Children’s Hospital. Dr Heraghty established the
hospital’s respiratory and long-term ventilation service. She collaborated with me in my PCAF and we
have a strong clinical academic relationship. Dr Heraghty and Dr Turcu will contribute valuable expertise to
the expert advisory group.
Host Organisation Support Statement
The following statement of support should be composed jointly by the Heads of Department / Senior Managers of
the employing host organisation and the partner organisation.
The Heads of Department / Senior Managers of these organisations will be able to add the statement of support to
the form once they have confirmed participation. Invitations to participate are sent by the applicant via the
'Participants and Signatories' section of the form.
Statement of support
On behalf of the acute speech and language therapy (SLT) service at Guy’s and St Thomas’ NHS Foundation
Trust (GSTT) and King’s College London (KCL), we are delighted to support Sabrena’s application for an NIHR
Doctoral
sectdars__001_p.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_q.text:
Clinical and Practitioner Academic (DCAF) fellowship. We confirm that, if the award is made, Sabrena will
be integrated into both departments enabling her to conduct clinical and research development activity appropriate
to the Fellowship.
There is a strong history of collaboration and partnership between the Evelina, Neurosciences department of the
Guy’s & St Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust (GSTT) and King’s College London (KCL) Faculty of Life Sciences and
Medicine (FoLSM), both committed to supporting Sabrena throughout her clinical academic development. Evelina
has previously successfully supported 8 PCAF and 5 DCAF amongst allied health professionals.
The School of Life Course & Population Sciences (in FoLSM) unites experts in women’s and children’s and
population health. Units of assessment within the faculty were among top performers in the 2021 Research
Excellence Framework. For Allied Health Professions, Dentists and Pharmacy KCL was ranked first out of 89
submissions in proportion overall percentage ranked at 4*, GPA and Power rankings. KCL is 7th in the UK’s top
universities and 35th in the world’s top universities in the QS World University Rankings (2023).
The acute SLT
sectdars__001_q.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_r.text:
service has a growing reputation for supporting staff in research development whilst developing
their clinical practice. At both a Trust and service level the staff in the service regularly collaborate with clinical
academics to conduct lectures, courses, and Continued Professional Development activities to ensure our
therapists are well informed and implementing the most up-to-date evidenced-based practice. We work with HEI’s
to support clinical placements and Sabrena is a practice educator. Sabrena has also participated in a wide range
of service development and audit projects including the design and production of an online dysphagia training
module for nurses, a vocal fold palsy audit and the development of an inpatient SLT service for patients with LTV.
Sabrena was also involved in evaluating a survey of parents/caregivers care experience of the inpatient SLT
service. This project is approved by the service and her proposed area of study fits in with Sabrena’s current
clinical commitments and interest. It will be of benefit to the SLT Service for Sabrena to undertake this research
project.
The KCL Florence Nightingale Faculty of Nursing, Midwifery and Palliative Care (‘The Fac
sectdars__001_r.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_s.text:
ulty’) is an integral
partner within KHP and contributes to the interdisciplinary research centres in delivering high quality research. For
the last three years, King’s has been number 2 in the world and number 1 in Europe for Nursing (QS world
rankings). The 2021 Research Excellence Framework (REF2021) placed King’s applied and allied health research
1st for all of the following: overall proportion of research rated 4*, research papers rated 4*, impact rated 4*,
environment rated 4*(joint), Grade Point Average and Power. REF2021 rated 100% of our applied and allied
health research environment, 86% of our impact and 70% of our research overall as world-leading. Over 95% of
our research overall was rated as world-leading or internationally excellent.
The Faculty’s applied clinical and health research develops, understands, implements, and evaluates
interventions, therapies, and care across our fields to build knowledge, lead policy and practice and improve
outcomes for patients and those important to them. We work with local services and our partner NHS Trusts, lead
national studies supported by NIHR, UKRI, EU and NIH and globally, and major research programmes in Africa
and Asia. T
sectdars__001_s.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_t.text:
he Faculty where Sabrena will be based, has a dedicated research support team led by Professor
Glenn Robert, (Vice Dean for Research and Impact). This team leads on implementation of the Faculty’s research
strategy and provides the infrastructure required to deliver tailored training to individual researchers. Sabrena will
also have access to all relevant modules and training provided via KCL and the Specialist Biomedical Research
Centres based within KHP, in addition to monthly grant writing workshops and research division meetings offered
by the Faculty.
Sabrena will work daily alongside Faculty researchers who are tackling some of the most enduring health issues. If
successful, Sabrena will become a member of the Applied Technologies in Clinical Care division. Faculty
academics have research networks through their commitments with national and international committees.
Working alongside these senior investigators within the Faculty and wider KHP will give Sabrena the opportunity to
learn more about designing and conducting large and complex studies and provides support networks and
mentorship for ensuring her findings have impact and are translated into practice and policy. Sabr
sectdars__001_t.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_u.text:
ena will learn
more about translating and disseminating research findings to directly inform practice and policy. All doctoral
students in the Faculty are actively involved with developing their research's impact, through engagement with
clinicians, managers, service-users and policy-makers at all stages, to prepare for the translation and
implementation of their research, from its original inception through to dissemination.
GSTT demonstrates a commitment to progressive academic study and clinical academic roles for AHPs. The trust
have appointed the first AHP Research Lead for the organisation, who has a position on the Trust R&D board.
The Trust has also just published its new AHP strategy with research as a stated priority, including supporting
clinical academic careers, hosting of clinical academic posts and commitment to supporting clinical academic
training.
As an organisation GSTT is fully committed to improving the experiences of all staff and patients, by providing
inclusive healthcare to patients, pledging to be an inclusive workplace and promising workplace equality. The
GSTT R&D strategic choices paper has prioritised equality, diversity and inclusive approaches to off
sectdars__001_u.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Training & Development and Research Support

sectdars__001_v.text:
er all staff the
opportunity for involvement in research design and delivery, and to meet the highest standards of research
integrity. KCL has both a Race Equality Charter and Athena Swan award and, through its Race Equality and
Athena Swan Action Plans, is committed to building an anti-racist University and to transforming and supporting
gender and race equality in research.

Item Description: FEES training course
Justification of Cost: Imperial College London hosts a Fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing (FEES)
training course. FEES is an instrumental assessment used for the evaluation of swallowing and is a useful tool for
ventilator-assisted children. Completing this training will allow me to improve my paediatric feeding skills and

Item Description: Overseas Research Visit to The Hospital for Sick Children in Toronto
Justification of Cost: In my 3rd year, I will complete a research visit to The Hospital for Sick Children (SickKids)
in Toronto. This hospital is Canada’s mo

secsb__001_m.text:
st research-intensive hospital and the largest paediatric tertiary centre in
the country. Their International Learner Program (ILP) involves observation experiences. Their multidisciplinary
complex respiratory care clinic provides care to children with MLTC-M who require ventilation support at home. I
will learn about their model of patient care for ventilator-assisted children, especially in the management of feeding
and swallowing difficulties. I will share my research outcomes and develop my relationship with the clinical and
research teams. I will also explore their current research endeavours and foster opportunities for future
collaboration. The minimum duration for an observation visit as part of the hospital's International Learning
Program is 5 days. I will therefore visit the hospital for 5 days. The budgeted cost below includes the hospital's
application fee for the International Learner Program ($200CAD / £116).
